notes: [Chapter argument reconstruction] Ch.17 argues that hope in Christ's literal, visible second coming has been the unbroken faith of God's people in every age — patriarchs, prophets, apostles, martyrs, Waldenses, and the Reformers (Luther, Melanchthon, Calvin, Knox, Ridley, Latimer, Baxter) — establishing continuity with the book's cosmic-conflict thread. The chapter then pivots to argue that Christ's own prophesied 'signs of the times' (Matt. 24, Rev. 6) were literally and observably fulfilled in the eighteenth century: the 1755 Lisbon earthquake as the 'great earthquake' and the 19 May 1780 New England Dark Day as the darkening of sun and moon, arriving exactly 25 years apart and dovetailing with the 1260-year prophetic period ending in 1798. This sign-fulfillment argument is the chapter's load-bearing claim: it converts abstract eschatological hope into an empirically-anchored countdown, directly priming the reader for the Millerite chronology developed in ch.18-19 (hence classified narrative_arc=millerite for the sign-cluster claims). The closing sections (backslidden church, Bethlehem parable) are homiletical amplification, not additional empirical load. [Impact stage notes] Defended.json carries zero items with a final effective verdict of contradicted, misquotation, or anachronistic: GC-17-036 and GC-17-040 (both anachronistic) were overturned to supported on defense (defense_succeeded, revised_verdict=supported) and are therefore NOT confirmed discrepancies — no impact object assigned, per the chapter-argument-collapse test they carry no residual discrepancy to score. The only non-supported items surviving to this stage are four 'disputed' claims (GC-17-042, GC-17-044, GC-17-049, GC-17-066), which per pipeline policy skip the defender stage; per task instructions these high-stakes disputed items received impact scoring below even though they are not 'confirmed discrepancies' in the strict sense. Scored: 4. Load-bearing: 0. No claim in this chapter, even at its highest impact_score, removes support the chapter's core argument cannot survive without: the core sign-identification (an earthquake occurred in 1755; a preternatural darkening occurred in 1780, 25 years later) is corroborated at high/medium confidence elsewhere in the file and is untouched by the disputed casualty statistics or the quay anecdote.

defense.argument: The anachronistic verdict rests on a category error the verifier itself half-concedes. White's word is 'pervaded an extent' — the reach over which the shock and its associated disturbances were PERCEPTIBLE, exactly Lyell's meaning. The verifier then measures that against the ~100,000 km2 heavy-DAMAGE (intensity ≥VI) footprint. Those are different quantities. Under the correct reading (perceptible reach), modern reconstruction corroborates an area of at least this order of magnitude. The academic ESI-07 study (Quaternary International, 2021) documents far-field seiches from the 1 Nov 1755 event in English lakes (Cumbria), Scottish lochs (Lomond, Ness), Swiss lakes (Geneva, Neuchâtel, Zürich), the Thames, the Elbe at Hamburg, and river outlets as far as the Dalälven (Sweden) and Aurajoki (Finland) — 'the observation of seiches as far away as Finland suggests a magnitude approaching 9.0.' USGS far-field tsunami simulations add the transatlantic reach to the U.S. East Coast and the Caribbean. A perceptible-effects region bounded by the Caribbean, Finland, and North Africa spans well over four million square miles (~10.4 million km2), so White's Lyell-sourced figure is a defensible LOWER BOUND on the perceptible reach, not an error 'wrong by modern standards.' The exact number is a period estimate that modern data corroborates directionally rather than a modern measurement, but nothing in current seismology contradicts it; the claim is defensible as stated. source_quality: academic-partial (search-snippet access to Quaternary International ESI-07 paper and USGS far-field simulation).

claims.quote: Mountains, “some of the largest in Portugal, were impetuously shaken, as it were, from their very foundations; and some of them opened at their summits, which were split and rent in a wonderful manner, huge masses of them being thrown down into the adjacent valleys. Flames are related to have issued from these mountains.”(480)
claims.claim_text: According to a cited account, some of the largest mountains in Portugal were violently shaken during the 1755 earthquake, split open at their summits, and reportedly emitted flames.

verification.rationale: This is a marked quotation from Lyell's Principles of Geology (GC footnote 480), itself relaying 18th-century eyewitness reports of landslides, ground fissuring and 'flames.' Modern geology attributes the phenomena to seismic ground failure, rockfalls and possibly ignited gases; the 'flames from mountains' language reflects period reportage rather than a modern volcanic/geologic description. Faithfully quoted from White's source; not independently confirmed as literal. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: The AD 1755 Lisbon Earthquake-Tsunami: ESI-07 environmental effects (Quaternary International)
1755 Lisbon earthquake — Wikipedia

defense.argument: Two points overturn the anachronistic label. First, the geological substance of the quoted passage is confirmed by modern seismology, not contradicted by it. 'Mountains impetuously shaken from their foundations, summits split and rent, huge masses thrown down into the adjacent valleys' is an 18th-century description of large coseismic landslides, rockfalls, and ground fissuring — precisely the ESI-07 environmental effects the modern academic literature documents for this event (scattered rockfalls in distal zones; permanent landslide/landscape changes at Güevejar and Minateda; ground fissures reported ~5 m wide). Second, the single anomalous element — flames — is explicitly HEDGED by White: 'Flames ARE RELATED TO HAVE issued from these mountains.' She asserts only that such reports existed (which is true; Lyell relays them), not that flames literally issued. The extractor preserved this hedge ('reportedly emitted flames'). So the checkable content of the claim is: (a) mountains were violently shaken and suffered summit failure with mass movement into valleys — modern-confirmed; (b) an account reports flames — faithfully and accurately reproduced. Neither component is 'wrong by modern standards.' The verifier's anachronistic call over-literalizes a hedged period report and ignores that the destructive-geology core is exactly what modern ESI-07 analysis records. The claim is defensible as stated. source_quality: academic-partial (search-snippet access to Quaternary International ESI-07 environmental-effects paper).

verification.period_historiography: 19th-century biblical scholarship commonly dated Joel to the pre-exilic period (9th-8th c. BC), consistent with the ~2500-years-before-1780 figure.

impact.reasoning: The 'twenty-five hundred years' figure reflects the traditional pre-exilic dating of Joel that was standard in 19th-century biblical scholarship; modern critical scholarship has shifted toward a post-exilic majority view, but the question remains an open scholarly dispute, not a demonstrated error, so severity is low-moderate and this is closer in kind to the anachronistic items (period-typical scholarship) than to a fabrication — the commentary should credit authorial method (reliance on the standard dating of her era) over invention. Centrality is low-moderate: the figure decorates the rhetorical parallel between Joel's ancient prophecy and its supposed 1780 fulfillment, but the chapter's substantive argument (that the Dark Day fulfilled Christ's own sign-prophecy in Matthew 24/Luke 21, tied to the 1260-year/1798 timeline) does not depend on Joel's exact composition date. Counterfactual: even if Joel were dated centuries later, the 'echo' framing and the millerite chronological argument built on the Olivet discourse would be unaffected; only the incidental antiquity-of-prophecy flourish would need adjustment.
